learning_outcomes.description: To offer a broad overview of the historical development in the art discourse of art history
To develop students' ability to set up a dialogue with a cross section of the world's major art historians
To provide an introduction to the critical vocabulary and concepts used by art critics and theorists

assessments.task_description: Worksheets with questions on aspects of the lecture and readings will be distributed and completed by hand, in tutorials. There will be 10 worksheets over the course of the semester, one for each tutorial. You only need to complete 8 out of the 10 worksheets to receive full marks in this assessment. Completion of each worksheet is worth 2.5% of final grade (maximum of 20% in total).
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.due_date:
31/08/2026 11:00 am

Exam to be held during regular lecture time, in regular lecture room.
assessments.task_description:
Students will complete, by hand, an examination covering content from weeks 1-5. The examination will test:

1) ability to identify key concepts and methodologies;

2) understanding of key concepts and methodologies;

3) application of key concepts and methodologies;

4) critical reflection on key concepts and methodologies.

Marking criteria and detailed exam breakdown will be provided via Blackboard and discussed in lectures + tutorials.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Exam paper to be completed by hand, in person, and submitted to course coordinator at the end of the examination.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.task_description:
For this assessment, you must choose one book from a list that will be distributed via the course Blackboard, and write an critical research essay/book review. Your essay must provide a critical reflection on your chosen book, considering the author's methodology in detail and presenting an argument that contends with the strengths and/or weaknesses of the methodology. Your essay must have a central argument, and must be supported by secondary research (in addition to the consultation of set readings and the recommended reading list, students must source texts independently so as to demonstrate independent research). A separate bibliography must be attached in Chicago style, and references must be done in Chicago notes-bibliography style. Word count does not include footnotes and bibliography, but it does include quotations.

Marking criteria and detailed task description will be provided via Blackboard and discussed in lectures + tutorials.

While this is officially/technically listed as an 'open' assessment, all students in this course are strongly discouraged from using AI technology in the completion of the essay. This assessment is designed to develop and test your own thinking and writing skills, essential skills in all arts professions. Outsourcing these essential skills to AI technology means that you will not be able to develop these skills over the course of your degree.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2 - Biography and Cultural Approaches to art history

Biography vs cultural approaches. This week, we look at classic early art historical texts that use biography as their methodology (Vasari, Van Mander), and compare these to the cultural approach advanced by Winckelmann (often regarded as the 'father of art history').

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 2 - Biography and Cultural Approaches to art history

learning_activities.topic:
Week 3 - aesthetics, Kant and Hegel

This week we look at the Enlightenment, and consider two key philosophers: Kant and Hegel. We consider these philosophers' very different approaches to art and aesthetics, and reflect on whether these methods remain relevant to art history today.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 3 - aesthetics, Kant and Hegel This week we look at the Enlightenment, and consider two key philosophers: Kant and Hegel. We consider these philosophers' very different approaches to art and aesthetics, and reflect on whether these methods remain relevant to art history today.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4 - Cultural History versus connoisseurship

This week we consider two very different approaches of the 19th century, Jacob Burckhardt's cultural history and Giovanni Morelli's connoisseurship. We place these methodologies in dialogue and consider their respective strengths and weaknesses.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 4 - Cultural History versus connoisseurship This week we consider two very different approaches of the 19th century, Jacob Burckhardt's cultural history and Giovanni Morelli's connoisseurship. We place these methodologies in dialogue and consider their respective strengths and weaknesses.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 5 - Iconography, Style, Formalism

This week we look at the great institution-builders of the 20th century, a watershed moment for art history as we know it today: Erwin Panofsky and Heinrich Wolfflin. Similar to previous weeks, we compare Panofsky's iconographical method to Wolfflin's formalism, in order to reflect on the strengths and weaknesses of each approach.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

learning_activities.topic:
Week 7: Post-colonial theory

This week takes us into the second half of the course, where we look at critical methodologies of the 20th and 21st centuries. This week we examine post-colonial theory, focusing on Edward Said's foundational text, Orientalism, and the early application of these ideas to art history in the work of Linda Nochlin.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

learning_activities.topic:
Week 8: Archival Workshop

This week we will be learning about archival research methods, with a guest lecture by a member of the Fryer Library team, followed by hands-on archival workshops in the Fryer Library during our tutorial slots.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

learning_activities.topic:
Week 9 - Culture, Culture Industry and Culturalism (Paolo)

This week, we have a guest lecture by Dr Paolo Magagnoli, examining "culture, culture Industry and culturalism" specifically in relation to the work of Terry Eagleton.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

learning_activities.topic:
Week 11: Interdisciplinary and Contextual Encounters (Andrea)

This week, we have a guest lecture by Dr Andrea Bubenik, who will be presenting on the work of great interdisciplinary art historians Aby Warburg and Michael Baxandall.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

learning_activities.topic:
Week 12: Visual Sovereignty (Dom)

This week, we have a guest lecture by Senior Lecturer Dom Chen, who will be presenting on the expanded theme of ‘visual sovereignty’ (taken from Jolene Rickard), to highlight some of the complexities of i/Indigenous art’s integration and reading within broader art histories and discourses, and key methodological contribution that have attempted to address/reconcile (wrestle!) with this from a more self-determined and culturally-grounded positioning/epistemological ground.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

learning_activities.topic:
Week 13: Where to from here...

This is a week for reflecting on the course, considering the whole range of methodologies we've looked at, thinking about what kind of art historian/theorist/critic/curator we each want to be, and completing final preparations for the book review assessment.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
